title: Authentication: passwords and JWTs
objective: See exactly how a password becomes a stored hash, and a login becomes a token.

body.h: Why the raw password is never stored
body.p: If the database were ever leaked, stored passwords would let an attacker log in as everyone, everywhere those users reused it. A bcrypt hash is one-way — checkable, never reversible.
A signed piece of text containing the user's id and an expiry. The server verifies it wasn't tampered with using JWT_SECRET, without looking anything up.

body.h: Why login timing is constant either way
body.p: If a user isn't found, bcrypt.compare still runs against a dummy hash instead of returning immediately — otherwise timing would leak which emails are registered.

lineNotes: bcrypt.hash(password, 12) — 12 is the cost factor; higher is slower on purpose, making brute-forcing expensive.
jwt.sign({ userId }, JWT_SECRET, ...) — anyone can read a JWT's contents, but only the secret holder can produce a valid signature.

quiz.q: Why does bcrypt.compare still run even when the user isn't found?
options: So response time doesn't reveal whether an email is registered
